ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Jalfrezi. The closest defensible parent is the generic curry food product class FOODON_03311737.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Vegetable dishes) is the closest parent category. No specific Jalfrezi code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Jalfrezi or vegetable jalfrezi specifically.

main_ingredients: mixed vegetables (bell peppers, onion, tomato, cauliflower, carrot, green beans)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Vegetable Jalfrezi is approximately 300 g, representing a single-person main course portion served with rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetable curry, home-made — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No specific Jalfrezi entry exists in ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable curry entry (per 100 g edible portion), which shares the same principal ingredients (mixed vegetables, oil, tomato, onion, spices). Macros have been cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entry 'Vegetables, mixed, cooked' and published recipe-level analyses. Energy calculated as 4×protein + 4×carbohydrate + 9×fat.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vegetable Jalfrezi. GI estimated at 40 based on the analogous entry for mixed vegetable curry (GI ~38–45) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The dish is dominated by non-starchy vegetables with a moderate oil content, consistent with a low-GI profile. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 23.4) / 100 = 9.4, using 300 g serving × 7.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~930 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a 300 g restaurant portion of Vegetable Jalfrezi may contribute approximately 930 mg sodium (~40% of the 2300 mg daily limit), and should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size accordingly.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) and high dietary fibre from non-starchy vegetables
type2_diabetes.guidance: Vegetable Jalfrezi has a low estimated GI of approximately 40 and a moderate glycemic load of ~9.4 per 300 g serving, making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when paired with a low-GI staple such as basmati rice or chapati in controlled portions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and high vegetable content with antioxidant micronutrients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Vegetable Jalfrezi is low in saturated fat (~0.5 g per 100 g) and contains no cholesterol, with a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio and significant vitamin C and beta-carotene from bell peppers and tomatoes, supporting a heart-healthy dietary pattern when sodium is managed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetable Jalfrezi (also spelled Jelfrezi or Jhalfrezi) is a popular Indian stir-fried curry dish originating from the Bengal region of the Indian subcontinent, now widely served across India, the United Kingdom, and South and Southeast Asian diaspora communities including Singapore. The dish is prepared by stir-frying a colourful medley of non-starchy vegetables — typically bell peppers (capsicum), onion, tomato, cauliflower, carrot, and green beans — in oil with aromatics including ginger, garlic, and green chilli, then finishing with a spiced tomato-based sauce seasoned with cumin, coriander, turmeric, and garam masala. The result is a semi-dry curry with a vibrant, tangy, mildly spicy flavour profile and a tender-crisp texture that distinguishes it from wetter gravied curries. Nutritionally, Vegetable Jalfrezi is a low-energy, high-fibre dish (~72 kcal per 100 g) dominated by micronutrient-rich vegetables. It provides meaningful amounts of vitamin C (~28 mg per 100 g), beta-carotene (vitamin A ~55 mcg RAE), iron, and folate. Its low glycemic index (~40) and moderate glycemic load make it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. The primary clinical consideration is sodium, which can be elevated in restaurant preparations. In Singapore, Vegetable Jalfrezi appears on Indian restaurant menus as a vegetarian main course, typically priced around SGD 16–17 and served with rice or Indian breads.
common_user_queries: Is Veg Jalfrezi good for diabetics?
How many calories in Vegetable Jalfrezi?
Jalfrezi vs Karahi — what is the difference?

confidence_reason: No exact Jalfrezi entry was found in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable curry entry and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central mixed vegetable data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for mixed vegetable curry. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable curry (2017) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central; no exact Jalfrezi entry exists in any approved primary source. 2. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on mixed vegetable curry data from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). 3. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN plant food entries. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as this dish contains no vitamin-D-contributing ingredients (no fish, eggs, or fortified dairy); confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as the dish is fully plant-based with no animal-derived fat sources. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as the dish uses standard vegetable cooking oil with no hydrogenated fat. 8. The input record listed cuisine_tag 'Butter Chicken' which is inconsistent with a vegetarian dish; this tag has been disregarded. 9. Serving size of 300 g is estimated based on typical Indian restaurant main-course portions; no standardised serving size was available from the source restaurant.
